name: Creusot — Prophetic Borrows in WhyML
summary: Inria's Creusot is an auto-active verifier that translates Rust MIR to WhyML using a prophetic encoding to handle mutable borrows in a purely functional setting. The `^x` operator denotes the future final value of a borrow, allowing Rust mutable references to be encoded as (current, prophecy) pairs. Pearlite specification language with ML-style logical syntax; Why3 multi-prover backend dispatches VCs via Z3, CVC4/CVC5, Alt-Ergo simultaneously.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Inria's **Creusot** is an auto-active verifier that translates Rust MIR to WhyML (Why3's IR; covered in the verification-IL family at `data/verification-intermediate-languages`), using a "prophetic" encoding to handle mutable borrows in a purely functional setting.

**Prophetic borrows**: the `^x` operator denotes the future final value of a borrow, allowing Rust mutable references to be encoded as (current, prophecy) pairs in a functional logic (RustHorn / RustHornBelt heritage). **Pearlite** specification language: ML-style logical syntax with ghost code, snapshots, and trait-aware predicates. **Why3 multi-prover backend** discharges VCs via Z3, CVC4/CVC5, Alt-Ergo simultaneously. Open-source v0.11 (April 2026); growing test suite with verified Vec/sort/binary-search/iterators; not yet shipping inside production-critical software.

sections.title: Prophetic encoding of mutable borrows
sections.content:
Creusot's central technical contribution is the **prophetic encoding** of mutable borrows, inherited from RustHorn / RustHornBelt. A Rust mutable reference `&mut T` is modelled in a purely functional logic as a pair `(current, prophecy)` — the current value at the borrow's creation, and the *future final value* it will hold when the borrow expires. The user-facing operator `^x` reads the prophecy of `x`, letting the specification refer to the eventual state of a borrow without separation-logic permission tracking.

The specification language **Pearlite** is an ML-style logic with ghost code, snapshots, and trait-aware predicates. Specifications are written in an `#[ensures]`/`#[requires]` style similar to Prusti's, but the underlying semantics is purely functional rather than separation-logic-based. This makes Creusot's proof obligations look like Coq/HOL goals rather than permission-bearing assertions.

sections.title: WhyML and multi-prover dispatch
sections.content:
Creusot compiles Rust MIR to **WhyML** (Why3's intermediate language; see `verification-intermediate-languages.json`). Why3's distinctive design choice is **multi-prover backend dispatch**: a single Why3 verification condition is offered to Z3, CVC4/CVC5, Alt-Ergo, Vampire, and Coq simultaneously, with the user choosing which prover to pursue per goal. This makes Creusot proofs robust to per-prover regressions and lets users combine SMT speed with interactive-prover precision on hard goals.

This contrasts with **Prusti** (`prusti.json`), which compiles to Viper and dispatches to Z3 only. The two tools target the same source language (Rust) with deeply different proof models: Prusti uses separation logic with automatic permission inference from the borrow checker; Creusot uses purely functional logic with prophecy-based borrow encoding.

sections.title: Status and deployments
sections.content: Status (as of 2026-04): open-source under creusot-rs/creusot, version 0.11 released April 2026. Growing test suite with verified `Vec`, sort, binary-search, and iterator implementations. Not yet shipping inside production-critical software; the toolchain is an active research project rather than a production verifier. Compared to Verus (Google kernel verification, Amazon deployments), Creusot is earlier in adoption but offers the multi-prover Why3 ecosystem that Verus's pure-Z3 approach does not.
